---
title: "Pandas Exercises: Phase 2 Supplementary Practice"
subtitle: "Real-world data cleaning and analysis with Sales data"
author: "Speedykom Group GmbH"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-block-bg: true
    code-block-border-left: "#31BAE9"
    css: styles.css
jupyter: python3
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Introduction

These exercises use **real sales data** from 2019. The data has real-world messiness: missing values, type inconsistencies, text anomalies, and unstructured address fields.

**Why this matters:** In real companies, data often arrives in **chunks** (monthly reports, daily logs, multiple data sources). Your job is to combine them and then clean them.

**Data source:** [Sales_Analysis repository](https://github.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis) — 12 monthly CSV files (Jan–Dec 2019)

---

## Exercise 0: Loading Multiple Files – Merging Monthly Data

**Option A: From Local Folder (File System)**

If you download files into a `sales_data/` folder:

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import glob
import os

# glob.glob() finds all CSV files matching the pattern
csv_files = glob.glob('sales_data/*.csv')
csv_files.sort()  # Sort alphabetically (Jan, Feb, Mar...)

if len(csv_files) == 0:
    print("⚠️  No CSV files found in 'sales_data/' folder.")
    print("   Download files from: https://github.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/tree/master/Sales_Data")
else:
    print(f"✓ Found {len(csv_files)} files\n")

    # Load each file one by one
    all_months = []
    for file in csv_files:
        df = pd.read_csv(file)
        month_name = os.path.basename(file)
        print(f"{month_name}: {df.shape}")
        all_months.append(df)

    # Concatenate all months
    df_all = pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)
    print(f"\n✓ Combined shape: {df_all.shape}")
    print("✓ Exercise 0 (Local Files) passed!")
```

**Option B: From URLs (Cloud/GitHub)**

Load directly without downloading:

```{python}
import pandas as pd

# List of month names for building URLs
months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

print("Loading 12 months from GitHub...")
all_months = []

for month in months:
    # Build URL: base_url + "Sales_January_2019.csv", etc.
    url = base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv"
    df = pd.read_csv(url)
    all_months.append(df)
    print(f"  ✓ {month}: {df.shape}")

# Concatenate into one DataFrame
df = pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)

print(f"\n✓ Combined shape: {df.shape}")
print(f"✓ Exercise 0 (URLs) passed!")
```

**When to use:**

- Local files: ✅ Production code, offline work
- URLs: ✅ Quick learning, easy sharing

---

## Exercise 1: Explore Merged Data

**Goal:** Understand what data we have and what needs cleaning.

```{python}
import pandas as pd

# Load all 12 months from GitHub
months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

# Combine all months (reusing the pattern from Exercise 0)
all_months = []
for month in months:
    url = base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv"
    all_months.append(pd.read_csv(url))

df = pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)

print("MERGED DATASET OVERVIEW")
print("="*60)
print(f"Shape: {df.shape} rows × columns")
print(f"\nColumns: {df.columns.tolist()}")
print(f"\nFirst 5 rows:")
print(df.head())
print(f"\nData types (all 'object' = stored as strings):")
print(df.dtypes)
print(f"\nMissing values:")
print(df.isnull().sum())
print(f"\nCompletely empty rows: {df[df.isnull().all(axis=1)].shape[0]}")

assert len(df) > 100000
print("\n✓ Exercise 1 passed!")
```

---

## Exercise 2: Remove Nulls and Filter Anomalies

**Goal:** Clean data by removing empty rows and header text that snuck into the data.

**Setup: Load and merge 12 months**

```{python}
import pandas as pd

months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

all_months = [pd.read_csv(base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv") for month in months]
df = pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)

print(f"Original shape: {df.shape}")
```

**Analysis: Clean the data**

```{python}
# Count header rows BEFORE filtering (for display)
anomalies = df[df['Order ID'].str[0:2] == 'Or']
print(f"Found {len(anomalies)} header rows to remove")

# Clean data using method chaining
df_clean = (df
    # .dropna(how='all') - Remove completely empty rows (all NaN)
    .dropna(how='all')
    # .loc[lambda x: ...] - Filter out rows where Order ID starts with "Or"
    .loc[lambda x: x['Order ID'].str[0:2] != 'Or']
    # .reset_index(drop=True) - Make index sequential (0, 1, 2, ...)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

print(f"Original shape: {df.shape}")
print(f"After removing empty rows: {len(df.dropna(how='all'))} rows")
print(f"After removing headers: {df_clean.shape}")
print(f"\nFinal: {df_clean.shape} ({100*len(df_clean)/len(df):.1f}% kept)")
print(f"Sample cleaned rows:")
print(df_clean[['Order ID', 'Product']].head(3))

assert len(df_clean) > 100000
print("✓ Exercise 2 passed!")
```

---

## Exercise 3: Type Conversion – Strings to Numbers

**Goal:** Convert "Quantity Ordered" and "Price Each" from strings to numeric types.

**Setup: Load and clean**

```{python}
import pandas as pd

months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

# Load, merge, and clean using method chaining
all_months = [pd.read_csv(base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv") for month in months]

df = (pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)
      .dropna(how='all')  # Remove completely empty rows
      .loc[lambda x: x['Order ID'].str[0:2] != 'Or']  # Filter header rows
      .reset_index(drop=True)  # Sequential index
)
```

**Analysis: Convert types and create Sales column**

```{python}
print("BEFORE conversion:")
print(df[['Quantity Ordered', 'Price Each']].dtypes)
print(df[['Quantity Ordered', 'Price Each']].head(2))

# Convert strings to numeric AND create Sales column using method chaining
# errors='coerce' means: if can't convert, replace with NaN
df_clean = (df
    # .assign() - Create/update columns in the chain
    .assign(
        # Convert 'Quantity Ordered' string → float64
        **{'Quantity Ordered': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Quantity Ordered'], errors='coerce')}
    )
    # Chain another .assign() for 'Price Each'
    .assign(
        # Convert 'Price Each' string → float64
        **{'Price Each': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Price Each'], errors='coerce')}
    )
    # Remove rows where conversion failed (NaN values in these columns)
    .dropna(subset=['Quantity Ordered', 'Price Each'])
    # Create 'Sales' column: Quantity × Price (now both are numeric)
    .assign(Sales=lambda x: x['Quantity Ordered'] * x['Price Each'])
)

print("\nAFTER conversion:")
print(df_clean[['Quantity Ordered', 'Price Each', 'Sales']].dtypes)

print(f"\nSales column created (Quantity × Price):")
print(df_clean[['Quantity Ordered', 'Price Each', 'Sales']].head(2))

assert df_clean['Sales'].dtype == 'float64'
print("✓ Exercise 3 passed!")
```

---

## Exercise 4: Tidy Data – Parse Addresses

**Goal:** Extract Street, City+State (combined), and Zip from messy "Purchase Address" column.

**Why keep City and State together?** Many cities have duplicate names across US states:

- Portland exists in Oregon AND Maine
- Springfield exists in Illinois AND Missouri
- Washington exists in DC AND multiple states

Splitting them creates ambiguity. Keeping them together (e.g., "Portland, OR") is the unique identifier.

**Setup: Load, clean, and convert types**

```{python}
import pandas as pd

months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

# Load, clean, and convert types using method chaining
all_months = [pd.read_csv(base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv") for month in months]

df = (pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)
      .dropna(how='all')  # Remove completely empty rows
      .loc[lambda x: x['Order ID'].str[0:2] != 'Or']  # Filter header rows
      .reset_index(drop=True)  # Sequential index
      .assign(  # Convert string columns to numeric
          **{
              'Quantity Ordered': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Quantity Ordered'], errors='coerce'),
              'Price Each': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Price Each'], errors='coerce')
          }
      )
)
```

**Analysis: Extract address components into separate columns**

```{python}
print("MESSY DATA (multiple values in one column):")
print(df['Purchase Address'].head(2).tolist())

# Extract address components using method chaining with .assign()
df_clean = (df
    # .assign() - Split comma-separated fields and strip whitespace
    .assign(
        # Split on comma and extract first part (Street), then clean whitespace
        Street=lambda x: x['Purchase Address'].str.split(',').str[0].str.strip(),
        # Keep City and State together (second + third parts after comma)
        # This preserves uniqueness: "Portland, OR" vs "Portland, ME"
        City_State=lambda x: (x['Purchase Address'].str.split(',').str[1].str.strip() + ', ' +
                              x['Purchase Address'].str.split(',').str[2].str.split().str[0]),
        # Extract zip code (last word of the third part)
        Zip=lambda x: x['Purchase Address'].str.split(',').str[2].str.split().str[1]
    )
)

print("\nTIDY DATA (one value per column):")
print(df_clean[['Street', 'City_State', 'Zip']].head(2))

print(f"\nSample unique City_State combinations:")
print(df_clean['City_State'].value_counts().head(10))

print(f"\nTotal unique City_State locations: {df_clean['City_State'].nunique()}")

assert 'City_State' in df_clean.columns and 'Zip' in df_clean.columns
print("✓ Exercise 4 passed!")
```

---

## Exercise 5: Time-Series Analysis – When Do Customers Order?

**Setup: Load and prepare**

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

# Load, clean, and prepare using method chaining
all_months = [pd.read_csv(base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv") for month in months]

df = (pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)
      .dropna(how='all')  # Remove completely empty rows
      .loc[lambda x: x['Order ID'].str[0:2] != 'Or']  # Filter header rows
      .reset_index(drop=True)  # Sequential index
      .assign(  # Convert types and create new columns
          **{
              'Quantity Ordered': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Quantity Ordered'], errors='coerce'),
              'Price Each': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Price Each'], errors='coerce'),
              'Order Date': lambda x: pd.to_datetime(x['Order Date']),  # Convert to datetime
              'Month': lambda x: pd.to_datetime(x['Order Date']).dt.month,  # Extract month
              'Hour': lambda x: pd.to_datetime(x['Order Date']).dt.hour,  # Extract hour
              'Day_of_Week': lambda x: pd.to_datetime(x['Order Date']).dt.dayofweek  # Extract day
          }
      )
)
```

**Analysis: Analyze orders by hour of day**

```{python}
print("ANALYSIS: Orders by Hour of Day")
print("="*60)

# Count orders by hour
sales_by_hour = df.groupby('Hour').size()
busiest_hour = sales_by_hour.idxmax()

print(f"Orders by hour (0=midnight, 23=11pm):")
print(sales_by_hour)
print(f"\nBusiest hour: {busiest_hour}:00 ({sales_by_hour[busiest_hour]} orders)")
```

**Visualization: Orders by hour**

```{python}
# Create figure and axes explicitly (OOP style)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

# Get the peak value for the reference line
peak_value = sales_by_hour[busiest_hour]

# Method chaining with detailed comments
(ax
 # .bar() - Create vertical bars for each hour
 .bar(sales_by_hour.index, sales_by_hour.values,
      color='steelblue', edgecolor='navy', alpha=0.7)
)

# .set_xlabel() - Label x-axis (which hour)
ax.set_xlabel('Hour of Day (0=midnight, 23=11pm)', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

# .set_ylabel() - Label y-axis (count of orders)
ax.set_ylabel('Number of Orders', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

# .set_title() - Add descriptive title
ax.set_title('When Do Customers Order? Sales by Hour of Day', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

# .grid() - Add horizontal reference lines
ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3, linestyle='--')

# .set_xticks() - Show all 24 hours
ax.set_xticks(range(0, 24))

# .axvline() - Highlight the busiest hour with vertical line (orange)
ax.axvline(x=busiest_hour, color='#FF8C00', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
           alpha=0.7, label=f'Busiest: {busiest_hour}:00')

# .hlines() - Draw horizontal line from y-axis to peak of busiest hour (orange)
# This helps students visually see the exact height of the tallest bar
ax.hlines(y=peak_value, xmin=-0.5, xmax=busiest_hour,
          color='#FF8C00', linestyle=':', linewidth=2, alpha=0.5)

# .legend() - Show legend
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

assert pd.api.types.is_datetime64_any_dtype(df['Order Date'])
print("✓ Exercise 5 passed!")
```

---

## Exercise 6: Product Analysis – What Sells Best?

**Setup: Load and prepare**

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
          'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/theAfricanQuant/Sales_Analysis/master/Sales_Data/"

# Load, clean, convert types, and create Sales column using method chaining
all_months = [pd.read_csv(base_url + f"Sales_{month}_2019.csv") for month in months]

df = (pd.concat(all_months, ignore_index=True)
      .dropna(how='all')  # Remove completely empty rows
      .loc[lambda x: x['Order ID'].str[0:2] != 'Or']  # Filter header rows
      .reset_index(drop=True)  # Sequential index
      .assign(  # Convert types and create new columns
          **{
              'Quantity Ordered': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Quantity Ordered'], errors='coerce'),
              'Price Each': lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x['Price Each'], errors='coerce')
          }
      )
      .assign(Sales=lambda x: x['Quantity Ordered'] * x['Price Each'])  # Revenue calculation
      .dropna(subset=['Sales'])  # Remove failed conversions
)
```

**Analysis 1: Top Products by QUANTITY**

```{python}
print("TOP PRODUCTS BY QUANTITY (units sold)")
print("="*60)

# Group by Product and sum quantities
quantity_by_product = (df
            .groupby('Product')
            ['Quantity Ordered']
            .sum()
            .sort_values(ascending=False)
            )

print("\nTop 10 products by quantity:")
print(quantity_by_product.head(10))
```

**Visualization 1: Top products by quantity**

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
top_10_qty = quantity_by_product.head(10)

(ax
 # .barh() - Horizontal bars (easier for long product names)
 .barh(range(len(top_10_qty)), top_10_qty.values,
       color='green', edgecolor='darkgreen', alpha=0.7)
)

# .set_yticks() - Position y-axis ticks
ax.set_yticks(range(len(top_10_qty)))

# .set_yticklabels() - Label with product names
ax.set_yticklabels(top_10_qty.index)

# .set_xlabel() - Label x-axis
ax.set_xlabel('Total Quantity Ordered (units)', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('Product', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_title('Top 10 Products by Units Sold', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

# .grid() - Add reference lines
ax.grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3, linestyle='--')

# Add value labels on each bar
for i, v in enumerate(top_10_qty.values):
    ax.text(v + 10, i, str(int(v)), va='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Analysis 2: Top Products by REVENUE (dollars)**

```{python}
print("\nTOP PRODUCTS BY REVENUE (sales in dollars)")
print("="*60)

# Group by Product and sum revenue
revenue_by_product = df.groupby('Product')['Sales'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

print("\nTop 10 products by revenue:")
for i, (product, revenue) in enumerate(revenue_by_product.head(10).items(), 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {product}: ${revenue:,.2f}")
```

**Visualization 2: Top products by revenue**

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
top_10_revenue = revenue_by_product.head(10)

(ax
 # .barh() - Horizontal bars for revenue comparison
 .barh(range(len(top_10_revenue)), top_10_revenue.values,
       color='darkblue', edgecolor='navy', alpha=0.7)
)

ax.set_yticks(range(len(top_10_revenue)))
ax.set_yticklabels(top_10_revenue.index)
ax.set_xlabel('Total Revenue (USD)', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('Product', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_title('Top 10 Products by Revenue (Total Sales in Dollars)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3, linestyle='--')

# Add currency-formatted labels
for i, v in enumerate(top_10_revenue.values):
    ax.text(v + 1000, i, f'${v:,.0f}', va='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Analysis 3: Summary insights**

```{python}
print("\nSUMMARY: Key Business Insights")
print("="*60)
print(f"Total unique products: {df['Product'].nunique()}")
print(f"Total orders: {len(df):,}")
print(f"Total quantity sold: {df['Quantity Ordered'].sum():,.0f} units")
print(f"Total revenue: ${df['Sales'].sum():,.2f}")
print(f"Average order value: ${df['Sales'].mean():.2f}")

# Compare top quantity vs top revenue
print(f"\nTop product by QUANTITY: {quantity_by_product.index[0]}")
print(f"Top product by REVENUE: {revenue_by_product.index[0]}")

assert len(df) > 0
print("\n✓ Exercise 6 passed!")
```

---

## Summary

You've completed the **data cleaning pipeline**:

1. ✅ Load & merge (combining 12 monthly files)
2. ✅ Explore (understand structure and problems)
3. ✅ Remove nulls (clean empty rows)
4. ✅ Type conversion (strings → numbers → calculations)
5. ✅ Feature extraction (parse complex fields)
6. ✅ Time-series analysis (temporal patterns)
7. ✅ Aggregation (product insights)

**Real-world applications:**

- Mobile money (MTN/mPesa): merging daily transaction files, handling missing IDs
- Market data: parsing addresses, calculating totals, finding top performers
- Health systems: consolidating clinic reports, identifying trends

**Data cleaning is 80% of real data science work.**
